system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 4: Module 3 - Painting by Numbers and Arrangement Examples
Pages 112-136 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

Painting by Numbers - Adding Chords to the Melody

It’s been quite a journey but well done for staying with the process.

This is where the rubber hits the road – and you’ll make your very first chord melody arrangement.

Over the past couple of lessons, we’ve had a close look at:

   How the chord progression of Fly Me To The Moon works and its key features
   How the intervals in the melody relate to each chord in the progression.

We now have everything we need to start applying chord voicings to the melody.

Let’s get into it!


Applying the Chords to the Melody Line

You may have been inspired by the genius of Joe Pass or Wes Montgomery and the creative and
virtuosic twists and turns of their chord melody arrangements, but let’s not get carried away (yet).

For now, keep it simple!

This final step is a straightforward process of applying a chord to the first melody note of each bar
and/or chord change in the lead sheet.

Let’s play the melody again now on the top 2 strings, but as you play through the melody imagine
where you would like the chords placed.

Generally, placing chord voicings around the start of each bar or at a chord change usually works
well.

Mark out these points on your lead sheet:


How to Work Out Which Chord Voicing to Use

Here is the simple trick for easy chord melody arranging:


 Choose chord voicings that have the same interval of the melody note as the highest note in the
 chord shape.


OR

a voicing that can be slightly adapted to have the melody note as the highest note and still sound
good.



To know which voicing to pick we need to establish:

1. The string the melody note is on
2. The type of chord that is in the chord progression whilst that melody note is played
3. The interval of the melody note in relation to that chord.

The best part:

In the previous module, we built up a handy collection of useful voicings, to suit each of the top 2
strings, chord type, and interval – everything we need.

Let’s use this dictionary now to find a suitable voicing for the first melody note I’ve boxed out – the C
note on the first beat of bar 1:


The first part is easy. This melody note is on string 1 – so we use a chord with the highest note on
the 1st string.

Next, let’s look at the harmony at that point. The chord played by the band would be Am7 – which is
from the minor chord family.

We can look in our chord dictionary we built in Module 2 and find appropriate voicings for minor
chords with the top note on the first string here:


So we are narrowing it down, but now we need to work out which of these minor voicings to use.

To work this out, we need to know what interval the melody note is in relation to the current chord
in the progression.

Looking at our interval analysis we conducted in the previous lesson, we see that this melody note
is a b3 interval:


So, we need to pick a chord from our dictionary with a b3 as the note on the top string:


But:

Our dictionary doesn’t have the chord in the key that we need.

Easily fixed – just shift the shape down on the fretboard so that the top note matches the same fret
where the melody note is on our score:

Now we can apply this voicing to our arrangement:


Once you get used to it, this process becomes as easy as painting by numbers.

Going through the rest of the first section of Fly Me to the Moon, I can harmonize the melody notes
I’ve selected by matching them with appropriate voicings from our chord dictionary like this:




   Play audio example 3-4


Making Arrangements More Playable

Don’t get too carried away adding too many chord voicings, like this:




   Play audio example 3-5




Although this can work as a special effect in certain contexts, generally you don’t need to place
chords on every melody note for a chord melody.

In fact, less is more – placing chords at key places ‘here and there’ is easier on the hands and is
usually more than enough to imply the harmony beneath the melody line. So don’t get carried away!


So let’s have another look at this phrase and cut back the number of chords so that there’s a few
more than when I first harmonized the melody, but not overdoing it:




   Play audio example 3-6




This is starting to take shape. But there’s more work to do.

In the arrangement so far, even though I’ve only added chords here and there, there’s one voicing
that I’m finding hard to jump to:


You’ll often have this situation in arranging – a balance between what you would like to do on the
instrument versus what is playable.

One of the best pieces of advice was from my classical teacher Tim Kain on this issue:


 “If it’s easy on the hands, the music will flow better – so most of the time making things as easy
 as possible is musically the best option anyway”.


We have some options here to make this passage more playable:

1. Cut out notes from the chord – e.g. we could cut that low B to make it easier to change to the
next chord:




   Play audio example 3-7




2. Choose a different harmonization approach – e.g. instead of block chords I could use parallel
octaves or diads (a “chord” with only two notes). Here’s an example of diads in 3rds:




   Play audio example 3-8




(Note: We’ll cover these kinds of approaches in later lessons of the course.)


3. Simply remove the chord and have no harmonization:




   Play audio example 3-9




I often find myself doing #3 most of the time.

Removing the difficult voicings completely often makes the whole passage work much better on the
guitar – it helps my hands to relax.

In the next lesson, I’m going to cover a useful way to instantly make your arrangements sound more
jazzy – by adding more tension on those dominant chords.


Adding Tension to Dominant Chords

The jazzy sound in arrangements usually comes from adding extensions and tensions to voicings –
particularly to dominant chords.

Although this phrase has a nice texture to it, it still has a somewhat “vanilla sound”:




   Play audio example 3-10




We can address this by adding a small amount of tension to the G7 bar.

As we covered in Module 2 – you can experiment adding tensions to the basic chord shapes.

This voicing is a good candidate for some tension:


Earlier in the course, I actually used this voicing as an example of the kinds of tension you could add
to chords.

Let’s add an Eb to that chord shape to make it a G7(b13) – the Eb is acting as the b13 giving it some
cool tension:




   Play audio example 3-11




See how just a small touch of tension can make an arrangement sound far more interesting.

In the next lesson, I’ll show you examples of complete arrangements using the process we’ve covered
so far that you can study as examples and starting points for your own arrangements.


Fly me to the Moon - Basic Chord Melody Arrangement

Here’s a complete arrangement of Fly Me To The Moon using the process we covered above –
matching appropriate voicings from our dictionary with the melody note intervals, and
removing/adapting voicings when I’ve found it awkward to play certain passages.

I’ve also added some slight tension to the dominant chords as we covered towards the end of the
previous lesson.

Although we’ll cover more advanced approaches later in this course, this is all you need to create a
great sounding chord melody arrangement that you can play along with a backing track or on the
bandstand (the backing tracks are included beneath each arrangement):

Alone Together - Basic Chord Melody Arrangement

Here’s another chord melody arrangement using the process we have covered in this lesson that you
can use as a case study of arranging with the chord dictionary method we’ve covered in this module.

Notice how I’m often cutting the lower tones out of the chords to make them more playable for this
up-tempo tune.

I also had to alter the rhythm of the melody quite a bit to enable the arrangement to be more natural
under the hands.

You can treat these arrangements as an example to learn on your guitar, or you could use them as a
starting point for your own arrangement in the lessons that follow.
